TRINITY · feedback-driven bubble evolution · detailed technical insights

The β–δ solver, the "cage,"
and WARPFIELD Problem 2

A full read of what was broken in the energy-phase solver, the mechanics of the fix, what freeing it revealed — and why the velocity-inflow scare it triggered turned out to be a non-issue. Equations, numbers, and source citations throughout.

Solver · hybr shipped (100% conv.) Problem 2 · CLOSED Open · Phase 5 transition criterion 2026-06-14 · verified vs. source on main@e15961e
TL;DR & timeline ▸ Executive summary ▸ The story in one timeline Part I · The solver ▸ 1 · The two state variables β, δ ▸ 2 · The self-consistency problem ▸ 3 · The three defects ▸ 4 · The four-arm bake-off ▸ 5 · hybr vs legacy (the payoff) ▸ 6 · The cage hides the inflow Part II · Problem 2 ▸ 7 · What hybr revealed ▸ 8 · The causal chain ▸ 9 · The hunt (6 configs) ▸ 10 · The counterfactual ▸ 11 · Is it physically real? Part III · Wrap ▸ 12 · Issues hit along the way ▸ 13 · Verdict & fix shipped ▸ 14 · Direction (Phase 5) ▸ Files & glossary

Executive summary

TRINITY's energy-phase solver finds two numbers each timestep — \(\beta\) (how fast bubble pressure changes) and \(\delta\) (how fast its temperature changes). The legacy implementation caged that search: hard bounds \(\beta\in[0,1],\ \delta\in[-1,0]\) plus a \(\pm0.02\)/segment drift cap. For steep density profiles the true root sits outside the box (self-similar theory: \(\beta_\text{adia}=3\cdot\frac{3}{5+\alpha_\rho}-1>1\) for \(\alpha_\rho<-\tfrac23\)), so the solver clamped to the edge and never reached it.

Replacing the grid-walk with a real root-finder (hybr) on a pole-free residual took convergence from 0% to 100% and let \(\beta\) range \([-2.44,+4.23]\). That, as a side effect, let \(\beta\) go negative during feedback surges — exposing a transient inward bubble velocity ("Problem 2"). We chased it across 6 configs and a deletion experiment:

0→100%
solver convergence (legacy→hybr)
909
segments swept · 100% converged
≤0.04%
macro impact of deleting the inflow
M≈1.8e-3
inflow Mach — utterly subsonic

Conclusion: the solver fix is real and large; the inflow it exposed is real but cosmetic for the energy/momentum budget. No treatment applied; a diagnostic tripwire v_neg_frac_thick now ships. The genuinely open problem these runs surfaced is the transition criterion (steep bubbles stall).

⏱The story in one timeline

Phase 0 · diagnosed

The solver wasn't converging

0% on mock & steep configs, 47.5% unconverged on a worked example; bounds/cap saturated; production integrated wrong-signed \(\dot E_b\) on affected configs (mock 16/50, simple 10/61, steep 8/56 segments).

Phase 2 · fixed

Four-arm bake-off → hybr promotes

Only arm D (scipy hybr on a pole-free metric) reaches the out-of-box roots (78–80% in shadow). Bounds widened; dMdt>0 + valid structure becomes the physical acceptance gate.

Phase 3 · validated

100% convergence, ~18× faster, profile-dependent physics

Self-consistent hybr runs converge 100% across all configs, \(\beta\) reaching 4.20. Legacy was profile-blind; hybr exposes that steep profiles stall and never cross the transition threshold.

Phase 6 · investigated & closed

Hunted the inflow, then deleted it

6-config sweep + reject-and-hold counterfactual: the inflow moves nothing material (≤0.04%). Problem 2 closed; diagnostic field shipped.

Phase 5 · OPEN

The real question that remains

The energy-ratio transition criterion may never fire for feedback-sustained bubbles. Likely needs a force-ratio / dynamics-aware trigger.

Part I — The solver

1The two state variables: β and δ

Each step of the energy-driven implicit phase must determine how the bubble's pressure \(P_b\) and temperature \(T\) are changing. Those rates are parameterised by two dimensionless numbers (the Weaver cooling parameters):

$$\beta \;=\; -\,\frac{t}{P_b}\,\frac{dP_b}{dt}\,,\qquad\qquad \frac{dT}{dt} \;=\; \frac{T}{t}\,\delta\,.$$

β — pressure rate

\(\beta>0\): \(P_b\) falling (normal cooling-driven expansion). \(\beta<0\): \(P_b\) rising — the bubble is being re-pressurised by a feedback surge.

δ — temperature rate

\(\delta<0\): cooling (the usual state). \(\delta=0\): \(T\) constant. \(\delta>0\): heating — rare, only during a feedback surge.

def. — get_betadelta.py:224 (β), :272 (δ→dT/dt). In practice the self-consistent runs span β ∈ [−2.44, +4.23], δ ∈ [−1.5, +2]; legacy clamped both into a small box.

2The self-consistency problem & the residual

\((\beta,\delta)\) are not free — they must make the bubble self-consistent. The energy injection rate \(\dot E_b\) can be computed two independent ways, and they must agree:

$$\dot E_{b,\beta} \;\xleftarrow{\text{Rahner A12}}\; \beta \qquad\text{vs.}\qquad \dot E_{b,\text{bal}} \;=\; L_\text{gain} - L_\text{loss} - 4\pi R_2^2\, v_2\, P_b$$

One side comes from \(\beta\) via the Weaver/Rahner relation; the other is the first law (energy gain minus radiative loss minus \(P\,dV\) work). A second equation pins \(\delta\): the structure solved at \((\beta,\delta)\) must reproduce the target temperature \(T_0\). So each segment is a 2-D root-find. The residual that measures the mismatch is where the legacy design bit:

$$\underbrace{f_E=\frac{\dot E_{b,\beta}-\dot E_{b,\text{bal}}}{\dot E_{b,\beta}}}_{\textbf{legacy — has a pole}}\qquad\longrightarrow\qquad \underbrace{g_E=\frac{\dot E_{b,\beta}-\dot E_{b,\text{bal}}}{L_\text{mech,total}}}_{\textbf{hybr — pole-free}}$$

with \(g_T=(T_\text{bubble}-T_0)/T_0\) and acceptance at \(g_\text{total}=g_E^2+g_T^2<10^{-4}\). The legacy denominator \(\dot E_{b,\beta}\) crosses zero near the \(E_b\) peak (where cooling overtakes injection), making \(f_E\) blow up. Dividing instead by \(L_\text{mech,total}\) — a per-segment constant — keeps the residual finite and well-defined for the whole phase.

A subtlety worth recording: in Phase 0 the f-pole was not yet operative (\(|\dot E_{b,\beta}|\ge0.14\,L_\text{mech}\) in every measured segment, zero crossings) — so the pole is a structural hazard, not the day-one defect. The g-metric was kept as hygiene, not the fix. The fix was the root-finder + freeing the box.

Edot_from_balance — get_betadelta.py:434 · g-metric denominator L_mech,total — get_betadelta.py:408, arms.py:103 · pole-not-operative — PHASE0_BETADELTA_BASELINES.md (Finding 4).

3The three defects (with the numbers)

① The ±0.02/segment drift cap

The search was a 5-point grid in a tiny window: β ∈ [guess−0.02, guess+0.02] (GRID_EPSILON=0.02). After a bad phase-handoff guess the true root outruns the window. On the mock config, \(\beta\) marched 0.76→0.24 at exactly ±0.02/segment for 19 consecutive segments, chasing a root moving ~25 β-units/Myr with a ~20/Myr cap. Result: 0/27 converged, residuals 2.4e-2…3.25.

GRID_EPSILON — get_betadelta.py:57, 969–972 · mock trace — PHASE0_BETADELTA_BASELINES.md (Evidence 3).

② The hard bounds — the "cage"

The box: BETA_MIN=0.0, BETA_MAX=1.0, DELTA_MIN=−1.0, DELTA_MAX=0.0. But self-similar theory puts the adiabatic root at

$$\beta_\text{adia}=3\cdot\frac{3}{5+\alpha_\rho}-1\,,\qquad \beta_\text{adia}>1 \iff \alpha_\rho<-\tfrac23\,.$$

For a steep \(\alpha_\rho=-2\) profile that is \(\beta_\text{adia}=2.0\) — well outside the box. In production the steep baseline pinned \(\beta\) at \(\text{BETA\_MAX}=1\) for 10 consecutive segments (reaching 1.42 as cooling pushed it higher), giving 0/56 converged. The four-arm root-map confirmed it: 19 of 21 converged hybr roots on the mock lie outside the box (\(\beta\in[-0.14,2.60],\ \delta\in[-1.51,-0.27]\)).

bounds — get_betadelta.py:41–44 · β_adia — BETADELTA_HYBR_PLAN.md:63 · steep pinning — PHASE0 (Finding 3) · out-of-box roots — BETADELTA_PHASE2_ARMS.md (Finding 1).

③ The f-metric pole (latent) & its consequence

Not operative in the measured Phase-0 segments, but the design hazard above. Its downstream consequence was real and shipped: where the balance-side \(\dot E_b\) goes negative while the β-branch stays positive, production integrated wrong-signed \(\dot E_b\) — mock 16/50, simple1e5 10/61, steep 8/56 segments — so published implicit-phase \(E_b(t)\) on those configs rose monotonically to the boundary instead of peaking inside the phase. (Paper-I caveat material.)

wrong-signed Ėb — PHASE0_BETADELTA_BASELINES.md (Finding 5).

A non-adiabatic consistency relation \(\delta=\tfrac{2}{7}(2\tilde\alpha-\beta-1)\) and an analytic warm-start predictor were both tested offline and rejected — the relation misses the solved \(\delta\) by 0.05–0.14. BETADELTA_HYBR_PLAN.md:74–83.

4The four-arm bake-off (Phase 2.3)

Four solver strategies ran side-by-side as shadow arms — same trajectory, zero production impact — on a mock (4e3) and a simple1e5 config:

armwhat it ismock conv.simple conv.median evalsverdict
A controllegacy: cap + box + f-metric0%47–50%24baseline
B metricswap to g-metric only0%50%25no-op
C free cap+boxiterated grid, wide rails0%60%37–121fails cost
D hybrscipy root(hybr) on g, physical gate78%80%10–29PROMOTE
Why only D works. B proves the metric alone changes nothing. C frees the cap but an iterated ±0.02 window still walks at ≤0.1/segment and can't traverse to a root a full unit outside the box — it burns the entire wall-time budget every segment. Only D's Newton-type step gets there. Crucially, every root D accepts already passes \(\dot M>0\) — so the artificial β/δ rails could be replaced by a physical acceptance gate (\(\dot M>0\) + valid structure). Gate G2: B and C fail; D promotes (78-pt margin on mock; 20 pts and ~3.7× fewer evals on simple1e5). Maintainer green-lit widening the bounds; hybr shipped behind betadelta_solver and is now the production default (legacy remains a selectable fallback).

arms — arms.py:144–196 · hybr opts (xtol=1e-8, maxfev=30, eps=3e-4) — get_betadelta.py:74 · Gate G2 — BETADELTA_PHASE2_ARMS.md:107–125.

5hybr vs legacy — the payoff (Phase 3)

On self-consistent end-to-end runs the difference is not subtle:

configlegacy conv.hybr conv.β reachtransition behaviour
flat (1e6, n=1e5)0% (0/74)100% (90/90)→1.63crosses 0.05 at 0.247 Myr
steep (1e6, α=−2)0% (0/74)100% (113/113)0.59→2.82stalls at ratio ≈0.35 (never crosses)
typical (1e6, n=1e3)—100% (151/151)→4.18crosses ~2.5 Myr
mock (4e3)0%100% (66/66)−1.04→4.23energy-driven

Two headline consequences:

  • Speed: hybr advances ~18× faster per wall-second — it short-circuits to 1 evaluation when already converged, where legacy grinds a full 5×5 grid with shrinking \(dt\). (580 s for 0.080 Myr vs 666 s for 0.005 Myr; ARMS:247–258.)
  • Physics: legacy was profile-blind — its clamped \(\beta\) contaminated \(L_\text{loss}\), so flat and steep both "transitioned" at ~0.097 Myr. hybr gives profile-dependent behaviour: flat transitions cleanly; steep stalls and stays energy-driven for >3 Myr. That is the door into Problem 2 — and into the open Phase-5 question.

6The "cage" doesn't just clip — it hides the inflow

A natural assumption is that the legacy box simply clips hybr's out-of-box root. cage_compare.py shows it's worse than that: at the WR-surge segment where hybr finds \(\beta+\delta<0\) and predicts interior inflow, the caged solver is forced to a different in-box root — a different \((\beta,\delta)\) pair with no inflow at all. The cage doesn't reveal-and-clip the anomaly; it substitutes a different solution that hides it. So Problem 2 was invisible until hybr removed the cage — it is a consequence of seeing the real root, not an artefact hybr introduced.

cage_compare.py · scratch/phase2/README.md (figure: rootmap_cage.gif / cage_compare.png).

Part II — WARPFIELD Problem 2

7What hybr revealed

A · The stall

Steep / low-mass bubbles never reach the transition threshold

$$\frac{L_\text{gain}-L_\text{loss}}{L_\text{gain}}<0.05$$

— every feedback episode (WR wind, SN onset) resets the cooling ratio upward (e.g. 0.44→0.67 at 3 Myr), so the bubble stays energy-driven for Myr longer than a monotonic criterion assumes.

B · The inflow (Problem 2)

When \(\beta\) goes strongly negative the bubble-structure ODE's velocity source flips sign:

$$\frac{dv}{dr}=\frac{\beta+\delta}{t}+\Big(v-\frac{\alpha r}{t}\Big)\frac{1}{T}\frac{dT}{dr}-\frac{2v}{r}$$

— pulling the inner velocity below zero (inflow), which the Weaver self-similar (outflow) structure cannot represent. The legacy cage clamped \(\beta\ge0\), so it could never produce this. bubble_luminosity.py:411

8Problem 2 — the causal chain

The clean identity (from the \(\beta,\delta\) definitions with \(P_b\propto n\,T\)) shows why \(\beta+\delta\), not \(\beta\) alone, is the trigger:

$$\frac{\beta+\delta}{t}\;=\;-\frac{\dot P_b}{P_b}+\frac{\dot T}{T}\;=\;\frac{d}{dt}\ln\!\frac{T}{P_b}\;=\;-\,\frac{d\ln n}{dt}$$

So \(\beta+\delta<0 \iff\) the inner gas is being compressed (\(n\) rising). \(\delta\)'s positive (heating) swing partly cancels \(\beta\)'s negative (re-pressurising) one — inflow appears only when the pressure-rise out-paces the temperature-rise.

stepwhat happenssteep 1e6, t≈3.23 Myr
① CauseSB99 feedback is time-variable → \(L_\text{mech}\) surges (pre-SN WR-wind bump ~3 Myr)Lmech,W ↑ ~1.8×
② Mechanismsurge injects energy faster than the bubble expands → re-pressurise + heatEb↑, Pb↑, T↑
③ Triggerβ dive (Pb-rate) outweighs the δ rise (T-rate) → net compressionβ −2.10, δ +0.99 → −1.11
④ Consequencethe \((\beta+\delta)/t\) source goes negative → inner velocity reversesvmin → −0.62 pc/Myr

Both the inflow band and the \(\dot M\) jump are downstream consequences of the same \(L_\text{mech}\) cause — neither feeds back. The \(\dot M\) jump actually leads the inflow (it responds to the surge first), which is why an early read mistook the surge's \(\dot M\) step for an inflow "kink." negvel_causation.md · dMdt–Lmech Pearson r≈0.95 with a hysteresis lag.

9The hunt — 6 configs, 909 segments

Six instrumented hybr runs spanned cluster mass 1e4→3e5, star-formation efficiency 0.01→0.30, and flat/steep/dense profiles, to find whether the inflow ever stops being cosmetic.

configsfemin(β+δ)worst v_minband (frac. thickness)verdict
h1 steep base0.01−1.11−0.620.74cosmetic
h5 long0.03+0.14—0.00cosmetic
h2 sfe100.10−0.42−0.160.31cosmetic
h3 sfe300.30−0.35−0.170.30cosmetic
h6 flat0.30−0.37−0.220.34cosmetic*
h4 dense0.10−0.27−1.330.72 (handoff)cosmetic
The headline finding falsifies the obvious hypothesis. "Stronger feedback → deeper surge → worse inflow" is backwards: the deepest inflow is in the weakest-feedback baseline (h1). Because \(\beta\) is a rate ÷ current pressure, a highly-pressurised bubble sees a given surge as a small relative perturbation, so \(\beta\) stays shallow. *h6 was the lone config to trip the analyzer, on its \(\dot M\) channel — which §10 then cleared.

10The counterfactual — does removing it change anything?

The decisive test: for each config with real inflow, delete every inflow segment (reject it, hold the last physical structure — the production hold path) and diff the trajectory against the accepted baseline.

config\(\dot M\) kicked (local)final ΔR₂final Δv₂final ΔE_b
h1 (deepest band)42.8%+0.043%+0.038%+0.022%
h2 (sfe10)11.7%−0.0001%+0.0001%−0.0002%
h3 (sfe30)9.6%+0.0000%−0.0000%+0.0000%
h6 (flat, the flag)12.5%+0.0000%+0.0000%+0.0000%

Even a 9.6–42.8% local kick to \(\dot M\) moves the final radius, velocity, energy and terminal momentum by ≤0.04%. h1 (the smallest, most sensitive bubble) shows the only nonzero effect; the big bubbles are ~0 (h6 differs by ~10⁻⁹).

"Why so small?" — checked, not hand-waved. Not a units artefact (deltas are dimensionless ratios — units cancel). Not a dead channel (h1's \(E_b\) genuinely deviates 0.63% during the band then re-equilibrates — so the held structure does reach the integrated state via the segment-start snapshot the ODE integrates; it isn't zero by construction). The smallness is physical: the band is brief (~0.15 Myr of a 4 Myr run), the bubble recovers, and \(\dot M\) (conductive evaporation) is a small term next to the cumulative \(L_\text{mech}\) injection and the swept shell mass.

11Is it physically real? (interpretation — flagged as such)

The dip flags a real astrophysical event — the bubble re-pressurised by the pre-SN Wolf–Rayet wind bump (verified against the SB99 input: \(\log_{10}L_\text{mech,W}\) rises ~0.25 dex / ~1.8× peaking at \(t\approx3.3\) Myr, with SN power exactly zero until \(t=3.61\) Myr, after the dip). But the inward velocity profile is most likely a quasi-steady-model artefact:

  • The Weaver/WARPFIELD interior structure is derived on the self-similar expanding attractor; feeding it \(\beta+\delta\approx-1\) (transiently anti-self-similar) extrapolates outside its domain.
  • It is energetically negligible: \(\mathcal M=|v_\text{min}|/c_s\approx1.8\times10^{-3}\) (with \(c_s\approx338\) pc/Myr at \(T\approx4.9\times10^6\) K), and kinetic energy \(\sim(v/c_s)^2\approx3\times10^{-6}\) of thermal.
  • It settles itself: the driver is transient (~0.15 Myr), the interior is too subsonic to sustain a flow, and \(v\) is absent from all three cooling integrals (so there is no positive feedback to amplify it). bubble_luminosity.py:696/745/785

Honest caveat (recorded in the docs): whether a real bubble has a small genuine transient inflow here is open — it would take a time-dependent interior solve to settle, not worth it given the ~10⁻⁶ energy weight. The physics narrative is committed explicitly as conjecture, not result.

Part III — Wrap

12Issues hit along the way

issuesymptomfix / takeaway
Wrong default solverprobes ran legacy (β≥0) → couldn't produce the dip at allforce betadelta_solver=hybr everywhere
BLAS thread thrash6 runs × 4 implicit threads = 24 on 4 cores; load 12.5, zero progresspin OMP/OPENBLAS/MKL_NUM_THREADS=1
Ephemeral containerlong background runs reclaimed mid-flight (no error, external SIGKILL)stream CSV rows (flush per segment); relaunch
Confounded heuristica first-difference "roughness" check flagged the surge as an inflow "kink"deconfound vs the surge ramp; the surge leads the inflow
Grid-size mismatchraw v_struct_nneg count meaningless on a ~60k-pt griduse v_neg_frac_thick (thickness fraction) — grid-independent
"Too clean" result0.0000% looked suspicious — units? propagation? gating?ruled out all three (full-precision diff + a positive-control attempt)

13Verdict & the fix shipped

Solver: hybr is the fix — 0%→100% convergence, \(\beta\) freed to \([-2.44,+4.23]\), ~18× faster; betadelta_solver now defaults to hybr (legacy remains a selectable fallback), with dMdt>0 + valid structure as the physical gate.

Problem 2 is CLOSED. The WR-re-pressurisation inflow is real but cosmetic for the energy/momentum budget. The accept arm stands — no treatment applied, because suppressing it would change nothing material and could erase real transient physics.

What shipped: a diagnostic-only snapshot field v_neg_frac_thick (registry + COOLING_PHASE_KEYS + a compute in the implicit-phase runner) — a permanent tripwire so any future configuration that does drive a deep, sustained inflow is recorded automatically. Full test suite green.

14Direction & open questions

The higher-value fish these runs surfaced is not the velocity inflow — it's the transition criterion:

Phase 5 (open). The implicit→momentum transition fires on \(\frac{L_\text{gain}-L_\text{loss}}{L_\text{gain}}<0.05\), but for steep / feedback-sustained bubbles that ratio is reset upward by every WR/SN surge and may never cross. The transition is modulated by feedback history, not a monotonic cooling-balance crossing — so a fixed energy-ratio trigger is likely the wrong criterion. The plan proposes a continuity-preserving force-ratio trigger:
$$\frac{4\pi R_2^2\,P_b}{\textstyle\sum F_\text{surviving}}<\mathcal{O}(1)$$

The 6 hunt CSVs already carry the cooling_ratio / Lmech / Pb data to start. Secondary thread: does a steep bubble ever transition once the SN epoch ends (~40 Myr), or is it permanently energy-driven in the model? A single extended-stop_t run answers it.

symbolmeaning
\(\beta=-\frac{t}{P_b}\dot P_b\)pressure-rate parameter; \(\beta<0\Rightarrow P_b\) rising
\(\delta=\frac{t}{T}\dot T\)temperature-rate parameter; \(\delta>0\Rightarrow\) heating
\(\dot E_b,\ \dot M\)bubble energy injection rate; conductive mass-evaporation rate
\(L_\text{gain},L_\text{loss},L_\text{mech}\)energy gain (=\(L_\text{mech}\)), radiative loss, mechanical (SB99) luminosity
\(g_E,g_T\)pole-free energy / temperature residuals; accept at \(g_E^2+g_T^2<10^{-4}\)
\(R_2,v_2,P_b,c_s\)shell radius, shell velocity, bubble pressure, interior sound speed
v_neg_frac_thickfraction of bubble thickness with inward (\(v<0\)) flow — the shipped tripwire
